---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size70[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size50[.center[

Evaluate two modern iterations of the Realist model in International Relations
]

+ Neorealism (Waltz)
+ Offensive Realism (Mearsheimer)

]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. ...

<br>

* Opening Discussion *

### Anything interesting going on in world politics at the moment?

<br>

### Everybody have the readings for today?

<br>

On Monday we diagrammed and discussed what can be referred to as a "classical" realist model of international relations.

To be honest, no modern theorists insist that the model we diagrammed on Monday is the most useful one.

However, I think it's super useful for our understanding to start with those classical roots.

### So, talk to me about the classical model?

#### - What are its key simplifications of reality?

(SLIDE)




---

class: middle, slidepurple

.size50[.center[**"Classical" Realism**]]

### Interests
.size35[
+ Men who want things
]

### Institutions
.size35[
+ Rules written by the strong
]

### Interactions
.size35[
+ Characterized by violence
    + Badly designed rules, not enough stuff or show signs of weakness = violence
]

???

### Strengths and weaknesses of this as our model of international politics?

### Any current events we could connect to this?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/05_2-putin_with_gun.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

.right[.content-box-grey[.size40[**Neorealism vs Offensive Realism**]]]

???

Today I want us to leap into the present day of realism.

From those classical roots have been developed a series of models that remain an important piece of the modern toolbox for IR scholars.

<br>

Today we'll dig into both of these models in order to:

1. keep practicing the exercise of diagramming and evaluating a scientific model, and 

2. expose you to two strands of modern thought that frequently come up in discussions of international politics.

<br>

*Split class in half, assign one half to each*

*Split half class into pairs to start*

SLIDE


---

class: slideblue

.size50[.center[**Neorealism vs Offensive Realism**]]

<br>
<br>

.size50[Let's diagram the revised models]

.size50[
+ Who are the key interests?

+ What are the key institutions?

+ What are the primary interactions that matter?
]

???

Ok, pairs build your diagram.

<br>

Now each half of the class, consolidate your diagrams and put them on the board.

<br>

Let's start with the neorealist model.


---

class: middle, slidepink

.size50[.center[**Neorealism**]]

### Interests
.size35[
+ Unitary states want security, not power
]

### Institutions
.size35[
+ International anarchy
]

### Interactions
.size35[
+ You must fend for yourself (threats are everywhere)
+ Security dilemmas dominate
]

???

### What does 'security, not power' mean?
(Power is the means to the end, not the end.)
- The end is survival

### What is the counterintuitive conclusion about power raised by Waltz?
(Too much power makes you a threat to everyone else!)

### According to Waltz, do men pursue power because they are evil?
(No! It's because they want things and things are scarce.)

<br>

### What is meant by a unitary state? Anybody ever heard that assumption before?

### Does it help if i tell you this is often referred to as the black box assumption?

(By unitary, we mean forming a single entity.)

The black box assumption.
- All of the characteristics of the state (e.g. wealth, regime type, voting system) go inside the box and the lid is closed.

### Since we cannot see inside the box, should we spend time measuring or debating what is inside?
(Nope.)

<br>

Neorealists focus on states as the key units embedded in the international system.

HOWEVER, what they care about is the placement of units in the system, NOT by reference to their internal qualities themselves.

Neorealism as a view of international politics with black boxes of different sizes running into each other on a map of the world.

<br>

### What is meant by anarchy?
(“...absence of a central monopoly of legitimate force.”)

### What does that mean?
(No world police.)
- monopoly: exclusive control of
- legitimate force: legal / allowable violence

<br>

So, unitary states who want to survive in a world of anarchy must view everyone and everything as a threat!

### Make sense?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_3-neighbors.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

.center[.content-box-red[.size40[**The Security Dilemma**]]]

???

Let's talk for a sec about the security dilemma because this is a SUPER important prediction about how states interact in the neorealist model of the world.

Just because it makes me deeply happy, let me briefly illustrate the security dilemma.

Imagine new neighbors move in next door to you.

You don't know them and you're not sure what they are up to so you decide to build a fence.


---

class: middle, slidepink

.size60[.center[**The Security Dilemma**]]

.pull-left[

```{r, fig.align='left', out.width='90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-Wilson.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

]

???

That seems eminently reasonable, right?

From your new neighbors perspective, they just bought their dream house and the day after they moved in their new neighbors build a fence and start watching them suspiciously.

How would you react to that?



---

class: middle, slidepink

.size60[.center[**The Security Dilemma**]]

.pull-left[

```{r, fig.align='left', out.width='90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-Wilson.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

<br>

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-dog_hatchet.png")
```

]

???

Of course, you buy a guard dog to protect you.

The big, mean guard dog makes you feel much safer.

No one can get near your house or your family without you knowing about it.

<br>

Unfortunately, your neighbor is terrified of dogs.

He didn’t like you but had no intention of ever going near your house.

He starts having nightmares about the dog, so...


---

class: middle, slidepink

.size60[.center[**The Security Dilemma**]]

.pull-left[

<br>
<br>

```{r, fig.align='left', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-handgun.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

<br>

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-dog_hatchet.png")
```

]

???

He buys himself a gun to feel safe.

You are now living next to a guy you don’t know very well who has been acting strangely since you moved in and has now bought a gun.

Your dog stands no chance against a gun and now you are having nightmares about being unsafe.

You decide the only solution is to up your security!

If one dog made you feel safe, then...


---

class: middle, slidepink

.size60[.center[**The Security Dilemma**]]

.pull-left[

<br>
<br>

```{r, fig.align='left', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-handgun.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

<br>

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-wolves.jpg")
```

]

???

A pack of ravenous wolves will make you feel safer!

<br>

This makes your neighbor absolutely crap himself in terror.

His small gun will be useless against a pack of wolves so he ups his own security...



---

class: middle, slidepink

.size60[.center[**The Security Dilemma**]]

.pull-left[

<br>
<br>

```{r, fig.align='left', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-tank.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

<br>

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-wolves.jpg")
```

]

???




---

class: middle, slidepink

.size60[.center[**The Security Dilemma**]]

.pull-left[

<br>
<br>

```{r, fig.align='left', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-tank.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

<br>

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_3-shark_laser.jpg")
```

]

???

This forces you to do the only logical thing you can.

Install a moat and fill it with sharks...

<br>

This is an example of an arms race created by the security dilemma.

### Everybody clear on the basics of a security dilemma?

#### - What is the fundamental lesson here?

(Purely defensive moves look threatening in a world of uncertainty and anarchy.)

- At no point did either you or your neighbor act with the intention of attacking the other person.

- These were entirely defensive moves only and they still spiral out of control!

<br>

### Do we buy this?

#### - Is this dynamic a good prediction for the world as you understand it? Why or why not?



---

class: middle, slidepink

.size50[.center[**Neorealism**]]

### Interests
.size35[
+ Unitary states want security, not power
]

### Institutions
.size35[
+ International anarchy
]

### Interactions
.size35[
+ You must fend for yourself (threats are everywhere)
+ Security dilemmas dominate
]

???

OK, summarize this all for me.

### Per the Neorealist model, how likely is cooperation?
(1. Cooperation super unlikely)
- Cooperation hard to start and even harder to maintain.

<br>

### What should be the most important part of every state's budget?
(Defense!)
2. States should actively seek to build up their own protections

<br>

### How should a state react when a natural disaster strikes in a faraway land? Send aid?
(3. Aid to people in desperate places unlikely, unless...)

<br>

### As a "map" of international politics does this leave anything important out?

Let's talk offensive realism!



---

class: middle, slidegreen

.size45[.center[**Offensive Realism**]]

### Interests
.size35[
+ Great powers focused on survival
]

### Institutions
.size35[
+ International anarchy
]

### Interactions
.size35[
+ Great powers have offensive military capacity
+ Attack may come at any time (uncertain intentions)
+ Weakness invites attack (rational opponents)
]

???

### Any questions on the key assumptions in Offensive Realism?

<br>

Clearly the big distinction here focuses on "greatness".

* ON BOARD *

### Let's brainstorm, what are the characteristics of a “great power”?
(pop size, nukes, etc)

<br>

### Could we rank these by importance?

### What does this ranking exercise make clear?

(Importance depends on context.)

- e.g. military strength in war, economic size in trade?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/05_2-justice-league2.png')
background-size: 80%
background-position: right

<br>
<br>
<br>
<br>

.size45[**Criteria to be a Great Power (Waltz 1979)**]

.size40[
+ **Size of population & territory**

+ **Resource endowment**

+ **Economic capability**

+ **Military strength**

+ **Political stability and competence**
]

???

Here is Kenneth Waltz’s criteria for qualifying as a "great power."

### Whose list is better, his or ours? Why?

<br>

### Can everybody see how this is a huge problem for this theory?
(No single definition of “great” or “powerful” will be appropriate in all contexts.)

### Given that defining a "great power" is so problematic, why in the world might someone prefer offensive to neorealism?

(1. Simpler models are better (assuming they are logical and consistent with the evidence).)

- Fewer moving parts means easier to apply.
- Need less information / time to make predictions
- Less room for measurement error to mess up how your model works

<br>

(2. The "great powers" aren't really that hard to define.)

- If you expand the list enough it gets hazy, but identifying the absolutely most powerful states isn't that hard to do.

<br>

(3. Offensive allows us to make different predictions than the others)

### What kinds of different predictions would Offensive Realism give us?

Offensive realism covers essentially the same ground as neorealism.

- Makes sense given the shared assumptions.

HOWEVER, it emphasizes "power maximization" as an expectation rather than just stopping with the security dilemma.

Remember, neorealists believe that accumulating too much power is risky because it makes you look like a threat!

<br>

So, offensive realism adds a concern with power maximization to our explanations of international politics.

### What is power maximization and why does it follow from these assumptions?
(The ultimate goal of every great power is hegemony.)

### What is hegemony?
(Mearsheimer defines hegemony as a state of total domination.)

So, according to Mearsheimer, every great power should pursue hegemony.

### Why does Mearsheimer argue that this compels them forward?
#### - Why not stop when you feel safe?

(1. Hard to assess relative power.)

#### - What does that mean? 
(I can’t tell precisely by how much I am stronger or weaker than you.)

(2. Even harder to project into the future.)

#### - What does that mean? 
(Across time I can’t know how our powers’ will change.)

### Does that make sense?

And I just want to show you, Mearsheimer understands the implications of his theory.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/05_2-mearsheimer-quote.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

This dude is hard core.



---

class: middle, slideblue

.size60[**For Friday**]

.size40[
Bring to class a recent international political event we can use as evidence to help us test the Realist models of IR.

Be ready to explain the event and make an argument about which version "best" explains what happened.

+ Classical
+ Neo
+ Offensive
]
